original_hypothesis.title: Any Token Holder Can Deploy ERC20 via withdraw() Bypassing Subject's onlyTokenSubject
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: withdraw() calls _deployERC20 directly when no ERC20 exists, bypassing the onlyTokenSubject modifier on _mint(). Any holder of a curves token can trigger ERC20 deployment with default name/symbol (or whatever metadata was pre-set). Once deployed, ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted prevents the subject from choosing different metadata.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This bypasses the subject's exclusive control over ERC20 naming. An attacker could force deployment with default 'Curves'/'CURVES' naming before the subject sets desired name/symbol.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw() calls _deployERC20 directly without onlyTokenSubject check; _mint has the modifier but withdraw bypasses _mint

preconditions: Attacker holds at least 1 curves token for the subject
ERC20 not yet deployed for this subject
Subject has not yet set name/symbol (or set them but not yet minted)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Any holder triggers deployment; subject loses ability to choose custom metadata
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No onlyTokenSubject check in withdraw's ERC20 deployment path

analysis_questions: Can the subject change name/symbol after ERC20 deployment?
What metadata is used — pre-set by subject or defaults?
Does this break symbolToSubject uniqueness in any way?
validation_requirements: Buy token as non-subject, call withdraw before subject mints, verify ERC20 deploys with defaults

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1) Subject creates curve (buys first token). 2) Subject hasn't set name/symbol yet. 3) Attacker buys 1 token. 4) Attacker calls withdraw(subject, 1). 5) ERC20 deploys with 'Curves'/'CURVES' default. 6) Subject cannot change — ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: No direct financial loss, but reputational damage and loss of subject control over token identity

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for ERC20 deployment via withdraw by non-subject

validation_result.mechanism: Access control bypass on ERC20 deployment via withdraw()
validation_result.impact_summary: Any curves token holder can force ERC20 deployment with default name/symbol before the subject sets desired metadata, permanently locking the token identity since ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted prevents re-deployment.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The withdraw() function at Curves.sol lines ~293-319 calls _deployERC20() directly when no ERC20 token exists for the subject, without any onlyTokenSubject check. In contrast, the _mint() function at lines ~283-288 applies the onlyTokenSubject modifier before calling _deployERC20(). The withdraw() function bypasses this access control entirely. When the subject has not yet set name/symbol via setNameAndSymbol(), the withdraw function defaults to DEFAULT_NAME ('Curves') and DEFAULT_SYMBOL ('CURVES'). Once deployed, externalCurvesTokens[subject].token is set to a non-zero address, and any subsequent call to _mint() or setNameAndSymbol() will revert with ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted or InvalidERC20Metadata respectively. The subject permanently loses control over their token's ERC20 identity.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal references lines 293-309 in Curves.sol which accurately describe the withdraw() function's direct call to _deployERC20(). The _mint() function at lines 283-288 clearly has the onlyTokenSubject modifier, while withdraw() at lines 293-319 has no access control beyond requiring the caller to hold tokens. The code paths diverge: mint() → _mint() (with onlyTokenSubject) → _deployERC20(), versus withdraw() → _deployERC20() (no access control on caller identity relative to subject).

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No onlyTokenSubject modifier. Any holder of curves tokens can trigger _deployERC20() directly, bypassing the access control enforced by _mint(). If name/symbol are not pre-set by the subject, defaults are used and permanently locked.
This function properly enforces onlyTokenSubject, but withdraw() bypasses it entirely by calling _deployERC20() directly.
Once ERC20 is deployed via withdraw(), this function reverts with ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted, permanently preventing the subject from changing metadata.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker buys 1 curves token via buyCurvesToken(subject, 1) — supply > 0, so this works for any caller
2. Attacker calls withdraw(subject, 1) — only requires curvesTokenBalance[subject][attacker] >= 1
3. withdraw checks externalCurvesTokens[subject].token == address(0) — ERC20 not yet deployed
4. withdraw checks if name/symbol are empty — if subject hasn't called setNameAndSymbol, they are empty
5. withdraw sets name = DEFAULT_NAME ('Curves'), symbol = DEFAULT_SYMBOL ('CURVES')
6. withdraw calls _deployERC20(subject, 'Curves', 'CURVES') — no access control check
7. _deployERC20 deploys CurvesERC20 contract, sets externalCurvesTokens[subject].token to non-zero
8. Subject later calls setNameAndSymbol → reverts with ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted
9. Subject calls mint() → _mint() reverts with ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: withdraw() at line 293 is public with no access control beyond token balance check. At line 298, when externalToken == address(0), lines 299-311 check for empty name/symbol and set defaults, then line 312 calls _deployERC20() directly. This bypasses _mint() at line 283 which enforces onlyTokenSubject(curvesTokenSubject) at line 285. Once _deployERC20 sets externalCurvesTokens[subject].token to a non-zero address, the ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted check in both _mint (line 286) and setNameAndSymbol (line 268) permanently prevents any changes.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this vulnerability. The defect exists in the core contract logic.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is straightforward and realistic: 1) Subject creates their curve by buying the first token. 2) Before the subject calls setNameAndSymbol() or mint(), an attacker (or any other user) buys 1 token for the subject's curve via buyCurvesToken(). 3) The attacker immediately calls withdraw(subject, 1). 4) Since no ERC20 exists yet and no name/symbol are set, withdraw() deploys the ERC20 with 'Curves'/'CURVES1' (with counter appended). 5) The subject can never change this — ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted blocks both setNameAndSymbol() and mint(). The cost is minimal (price of 1 token on the bonding curve at low supply + gas). The attack can be automated to front-run any setNameAndSymbol transaction in the mempool.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. No direct financial loss occurs, but the subject permanently loses control over their ERC20 token's identity (name and symbol). This is a permanent, irreversible state change that affects the subject's branding and token discoverability. All subjects' ERC20 tokens would end up with generic 'Curves N' / 'CURVESN' names. The symbolToSubject mapping is also set with the default symbol, potentially causing collisions or confusion. In a protocol where token identity and branding are important for subjects, this is a meaningful loss of control.
recommended_mitigations: Add onlyTokenSubject(curvesTokenSubject) modifier to withdraw() for the ERC20 deployment path, or require that the ERC20 has already been deployed before allowing withdraw
Separate the ERC20 deployment from the withdraw flow — require subjects to explicitly deploy via mint() before anyone can withdraw
Add a check in withdraw(): if (externalToken == address(0)) revert TokenAbsentForCurvesTokenSubject(); — requiring the subject to deploy ERC20 first
Alternative: allow the subject to update name/symbol even after ERC20 deployment (would require CurvesERC20 to support name/symbol changes)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Curves, CurvesERC20Factory, FeeSplitter. 2. Subject (address A) calls buyCurvesToken(A, 1) to create curve. 3. Attacker (address B) calls buyCurvesToken(A, 1) to buy 1 token. 4. Attacker calls withdraw(A, 1). 5. Verify externalCurvesTokens[A].token != address(0). 6. Verify name is 'Curves 1' and symbol is 'CURVES1'. 7. Subject calls setNameAndSymbol(A, 'MyToken', 'MTK') — expect revert with ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted. 8. Subject calls mint(A) — expect revert with ERC20TokenAlreadyMinted.
uncertainties: If the subject uses buyCurvesTokenWithName to simultaneously buy and deploy ERC20, the race condition window is eliminated for that specific flow. However, subjects using buyCurvesToken + later setNameAndSymbol + mint are vulnerable.
The severity depends on how important token naming is to the protocol's business model
additional_testing_needed: Test withdraw() by non-subject when subject has pre-set name/symbol via setNameAndSymbol — verify the pre-set metadata is used (still a bypass but less impactful)
Test the interaction with symbolToSubject uniqueness — can an attacker force deployment with a symbol that blocks another subject's desired symbol?
Test front-running scenarios: attacker monitors mempool for setNameAndSymbol transactions and front-runs with withdraw()
